challenges.title: Payments Processing
challenges.scenario: A fintech startup needs to process credit card payments for their e-commerce platform. The system must handle up to 5,000 transactions per second during peak shopping events, provide sub-100ms payment authorization, and log every transaction for a full audit trail. All cardholder data must be handled in a PCI DSS-compliant manner.

hints.text: Think about how to decouple payment intake from downstream processing to absorb burst traffic without data loss. What does your entry point look like, and how do you prevent overloading your processing layer?

Managed serverless services reduce your PCI DSS attestation surface considerably. Consider which AWS services are already certified under PCI DSS and how you can avoid storing raw card data in your own storage layer.

Use API Gateway + Lambda for the payment intake layer, DynamoDB for transaction state, and SQS for reliable async downstream processing. KMS encrypts sensitive fields at rest and Secrets Manager holds third-party payment gateway credentials.

answer.summary: A serverless payment pipeline uses API Gateway to accept transactions, Lambda for validation and routing, SQS to buffer and decouple downstream processing, and DynamoDB to store transaction state with encryption at rest. This keeps the PCI DSS attestation scope narrow by leveraging AWS-managed PCI-certified services throughout.

optionalVariants.title: SQS FIFO for Ordered Processing
optionalVariants.description: Replace the standard SQS queue with FIFO to guarantee per-merchant message ordering and exactly-once delivery — at the cost of lower throughput (3,000 TPS cap) and higher price per message.

optionalVariants.title: Aurora Instead of DynamoDB
optionalVariants.description: Use Amazon Aurora Serverless for transaction storage if your team needs complex SQL queries across the audit trail; provides relational structure but is harder to scale under sudden burst traffic.

tradeoffs: Lambda cold starts can add latency on the first request — use provisioned concurrency on the authorization function to guarantee sub-100ms p99.
DynamoDB's eventually consistent reads are cheaper but can return stale data — use strongly consistent reads for idempotency checks.
SQS standard queues offer at-least-once delivery — implement idempotent consumers to handle rare duplicate messages safely.
answer.resilienceNotes: SQS dead-letter queues capture any failed transaction messages for reprocessing. DynamoDB point-in-time recovery provides continuous backup. Lambda automatically retries on transient failures. API Gateway is multi-AZ by default.
answer.securityNotes: KMS customer-managed keys encrypt all DynamoDB data at rest. Secrets Manager rotates payment gateway API credentials automatically. Lambda functions run in a VPC with no inbound internet access. API Gateway resource policies restrict access to known IP ranges during testing.
answer.whyItFits: High Volume: SQS absorbs burst traffic and Lambda scales concurrently without pre-provisioning. PCI Compliance: all services are PCI DSS-certified; card data never touches self-managed infrastructure. Low Latency: provisioned concurrency on Lambda eliminates cold starts; DynamoDB single-digit millisecond reads keep authorization fast.
answer.diagram:
graph LR
    Client([Client])-->APIGW[API Gateway]
    APIGW-->Validate[Lambda]
    Validate-->KMS[KMS]
    Validate-->DDB[(DynamoDB)]
    Validate-->SQS[SQS]
    SQS-->Process[Lambda]
    Process-->SM[Secrets Manager]
    Process-->DDB

challenges.title: Analytics Ingestion
challenges.scenario: A media streaming company wants to collect real-time user behaviour events — play, pause, seek, and quality changes — from their platform. They expect up to 100,000 events per second at peak and need all data available for SQL-based analysis within 60 seconds of ingestion. The analytics team does not manage servers.

hints.text: Think about the difference between streaming and batching for high-volume event ingest. At 100,000 events per second, pulling from a queue is too slow — what pattern lets you process a continuous stream of records in parallel?

AWS has a managed service purpose-built for ingesting high-throughput real-time data streams. It can fan out to multiple consumers simultaneously, including one that automatically delivers batches to durable storage without writing any code.

Use Kinesis Data Streams for real-time ingest, Kinesis Data Firehose to buffer and deliver to S3 with a 60-second flush interval, Glue Data Catalog to register the schema, and Athena for serverless SQL queries directly on S3.

answer.summary: Events flow from clients into Kinesis Data Streams, which provides durable buffering and parallel consumption. Kinesis Data Firehose automatically batches and delivers events to S3 within 60 seconds. The Glue Data Catalog registers the schema so Athena can run SQL queries directly against S3 without a database cluster.

optionalVariants.title: Amazon MSK Instead of Kinesis
optionalVariants.description: Use Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (MSK) in place of Kinesis Data Streams if your team already knows Kafka; supports more consumer groups but requires more operational configuration.

optionalVariants.title: Lambda Consumer Without Firehose
optionalVariants.description: Attach a Lambda function to the Kinesis stream to transform and write records directly to S3 without Firehose; gives full control over partitioning and format but requires writing and maintaining delivery code.

tradeoffs: Firehose's 60-second buffer satisfies the latency requirement but increases cost if the window is reduced — tune buffer size and interval together.
Kinesis Data Streams shard capacity requires upfront planning; use enhanced fan-out if multiple consumers need to read the same stream independently.
Athena query performance depends heavily on S3 partition strategy — partition by date and event type to avoid full-bucket scans.
answer.resilienceNotes: Kinesis Data Streams retains records for 24 hours by default (extendable to 7 days), allowing replay if a consumer fails. Firehose automatically retries S3 delivery on transient failures. S3 provides eleven nines of durability.
answer.securityNotes: Enable server-side encryption on the Kinesis stream and S3 bucket using KMS. Restrict Glue Data Catalog access with IAM resource-based policies. Use Athena workgroup settings to enforce query result encryption and cost controls.
answer.whyItFits: High Volume: Kinesis Data Streams scales to hundreds of shards to absorb 100,000 events per second. Near Real-Time: Firehose's 60-second delivery window meets the SLA; Glue and Athena make data queryable immediately after each S3 object lands.
answer.diagram:
graph LR
    Clients([Platform Clients])-->KDS[Kinesis Data Streams]
    KDS-->FH[Kinesis Firehose]
    FH-->S3[(S3)]
    S3-->Glue[Glue Data Catalog]
    Glue-->Athena[Athena]

challenges.title: Event-Driven Employee Onboarding
challenges.scenario: An HR software company needs to automate their employee onboarding workflow. When HR confirms a new hire, the system must trigger document generation, IT account provisioning, benefits enrolment notification, and a welcome email — all within five minutes. The engineering team is small and wants to avoid managing servers or complex infrastructure.

hints.text: Think about how to decouple the triggering event from the downstream processes. If one downstream step fails, the others should not be affected. What pattern separates the trigger from the consumers?

When multiple independent services need to react to the same business event, a pub/sub or event bus model works well. AWS has a fully managed serverless event bus that requires no infrastructure management and can route events to multiple targets simultaneously.

EventBridge receives the new-hire event and routes it to a Step Functions workflow for orchestration. Each onboarding step (document generation, IT provisioning, benefits enrolment) runs as a separate Lambda function, with SNS or SES handling the welcome notification.

answer.summary: A new-hire confirmation event is published to EventBridge, which routes it to an AWS Step Functions workflow. Step Functions orchestrates the onboarding steps in parallel where possible — document generation, IT provisioning, and benefits enrolment — each implemented as a Lambda function. SES sends the welcome email as the final step.

optionalVariants.title: SNS Fan-Out Without EventBridge
optionalVariants.description: Publish the new-hire event directly to SNS and fan out to separate SQS queues per step; simpler and slightly cheaper than EventBridge, but loses content-based routing rules and event schema validation.

optionalVariants.title: Step Functions Express Workflow
optionalVariants.description: Use Step Functions Express Workflows instead of Standard for lower cost per execution; Express does not guarantee exactly-once execution, so each Lambda step must be idempotent to handle rare duplicates safely.

tradeoffs: Step Functions Standard workflows provide a full audit trail and support long-running executions but cost more per state transition than Express workflows.
Step Functions Express workflows are cheaper and faster for short-lived orchestration but do not guarantee exactly-once execution.
Using SQS between EventBridge and Lambda adds a small delivery delay but provides a buffer and dead-letter queue for failed steps.
answer.resilienceNotes: Step Functions has built-in retry policies with configurable backoff for each task state. SQS dead-letter queues capture any failed messages for investigation. Lambda automatically retries on transient errors. EventBridge replays failed event deliveries up to 24 hours.
answer.securityNotes: Each Lambda function uses a least-privilege IAM role scoped only to the resources it needs. Secrets Manager stores credentials for third-party HR APIs. SES sending identities are verified to prevent spoofing.
answer.whyItFits: Operational Simplicity: EventBridge, Step Functions, Lambda, and SES are all fully managed with zero server provisioning or patching. Cost Sensitive: the entire workflow is pay-per-execution — the system costs near zero when no hires are being onboarded.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    HR([HR System])-->EB[EventBridge]
    EB-->SF[Step Functions]
    SF-->L1[Lambda: Docs]
    SF-->L2[Lambda: IT]
    SF-->L3[Lambda: Benefits]
    SF-->SES[SES]

challenges.title: Video Transcoding Pipeline
challenges.scenario: A content platform allows creators to upload videos up to 4 GB in size. Each uploaded video must be automatically transcoded into four resolutions (4K, 1080p, 720p, and 480p) for adaptive streaming. Upload volumes spike sharply during daytime and drop to near zero overnight. The company needs to optimise for cost while keeping transcoding latency under 30 minutes per video.

hints.text: Think about how object storage events can trigger asynchronous processing without polling. What happens when a video lands in storage — how does the rest of the pipeline know there is work to do?

AWS has a fully managed service purpose-built for video transcoding at scale that removes the need to manage FFmpeg on EC2. For cost optimisation, consider how a queue between storage and the transcoder lets you absorb upload spikes without over-provisioning.

An S3 upload triggers an SQS message. A Lambda function reads the queue and submits a transcoding job to AWS Elemental MediaConvert with output presets for each resolution. Transcoded outputs are written back to a separate S3 bucket for delivery.

answer.summary: Creators upload videos to an S3 input bucket. An S3 event notification publishes a message to SQS. A Lambda function polls the queue and submits a job to AWS Elemental MediaConvert with four output presets. MediaConvert writes transcoded renditions to an S3 output bucket, and SNS notifies downstream systems when the job completes.

optionalVariants.title: ECS Fargate with Custom FFmpeg
optionalVariants.description: Replace MediaConvert with ECS Fargate running a custom FFmpeg container for fine-grained codec and filter control; adds ongoing container maintenance overhead and requires FFmpeg expertise.

optionalVariants.title: AWS Batch with Spot Instances
optionalVariants.description: Route overnight transcoding jobs to AWS Batch using Spot Instances for up to 70% cost reduction; Spot interruptions can delay jobs, so set a maximum wait time and fall back to On-Demand when deadlines are firm.

tradeoffs: MediaConvert pricing is per minute of video output — for very large libraries, EC2 Spot with FFmpeg may be cheaper at the cost of operational complexity.
SQS visibility timeout must exceed the maximum expected transcoding duration to prevent jobs from being delivered twice.
S3 Transfer Acceleration can speed up global uploads but adds cost per GB transferred.
answer.resilienceNotes: SQS visibility timeout and message retention prevent job loss if a Lambda invocation fails. MediaConvert automatically retries transient failures. S3 event notifications are at-least-once — Lambda handlers must be idempotent. S3 versioning on the output bucket protects against accidental overwrites.
answer.securityNotes: S3 bucket policies block public access on both input and output buckets. IAM roles for Lambda and MediaConvert follow least privilege. Pre-signed S3 URLs are used for secure creator uploads without exposing bucket credentials. KMS encrypts stored video files at rest.
answer.whyItFits: High Volume: SQS decouples uploads from transcoding, preventing the pipeline from being overwhelmed during upload spikes. MediaConvert scales to thousands of concurrent jobs without capacity planning. Cost Sensitive: the serverless and managed pipeline charges only for what is used — zero cost overnight when no uploads occur.
answer.diagram:
graph LR
    Upload([Creator])-->S3In[(S3 Input)]
    S3In-->|Event|SQS[SQS]
    SQS-->Lambda[Lambda]
    Lambda-->MC[MediaConvert]
    MC-->S3Out[(S3 Output)]
    MC-->SNS[SNS]

challenges.title: Global Customer Support Platform
challenges.scenario: A global SaaS company wants to build a customer support platform that handles voice calls, live chat, and email cases across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. The system must automatically route cases to available agents based on skill and language. It must continue operating even if a single AWS region becomes fully unavailable.

hints.text: Think about what routing strategies allow the system to automatically fail over to a healthy region without manual intervention. How would customers reach the system if the region they normally connect to goes down?

AWS has a managed contact centre service that handles voice, chat, and task routing in a single platform without building a telephony stack. For cross-region resilience, think about what makes database replication across regions easy without managing read replicas manually.

Deploy Amazon Connect instances in two regions. Use Route 53 health-check-based failover to redirect inbound traffic to the healthy region. DynamoDB Global Tables replicate agent state and case data across regions automatically with multi-master writes.

answer.summary: Amazon Connect instances are deployed in two AWS regions. Route 53 health checks monitor each region's Connect endpoint and automatically fail over DNS to the healthy region within 60 seconds. DynamoDB Global Tables replicate case records and agent state across both regions with multi-master writes, ensuring agents in both regions always have current data.

optionalVariants.title: Pilot Light Standby Region
optionalVariants.description: Run a minimal standby Connect instance in a second region and promote it manually during a regional outage; cheaper than active-active but requires human intervention and accepts a few minutes of downtime.

optionalVariants.title: Third-Party Contact Centre on AWS
optionalVariants.description: Deploy Genesys, NICE CXone, or Cisco Webex on EC2 or ECS if your team already operates those platforms; preserves existing agent workflows but shifts infrastructure management and patching back to your team.

tradeoffs: DynamoDB Global Tables use eventual consistency across regions — agents in different regions may briefly see slightly different state during a failover window.
Amazon Connect contact flows must be independently configured in each region — changes must be deployed to both regions to stay in sync.
Route 53 DNS TTL must be set low (30–60 seconds) to minimise failover time, which increases DNS query volume and cost slightly.
answer.resilienceNotes: Route 53 health checks detect regional failures within 10–30 seconds and reroute DNS automatically. DynamoDB Global Tables provide active-active replication with automatic conflict resolution. Lambda functions are deployed independently in each region and do not share state between regions.
answer.securityNotes: Amazon Connect uses IAM for agent authentication and resource-based policies for instance isolation. Call recordings are encrypted with KMS and stored in S3. HIPAA and PCI eligibility is available on Connect — verify applicable compliance requirements for the use case. VPC endpoints prevent Lambda from traversing the public internet when calling DynamoDB.
answer.whyItFits: Global Accessibility: Connect instances in multiple regions reduce latency for agents and callers in each geography. Multi-Region Resilience: Route 53 health-check failover and DynamoDB Global Tables ensure the platform continues operating even if one region is completely unavailable.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    Customer([Customer])-->R53[Route 53]
    R53-->C1[Connect Region 1]
    R53-->C2[Connect Region 2]
    C1-->L1[Lambda]
    C2-->L2[Lambda]
    L1-->DDB[(DynamoDB Global Tables)]
    L2-->DDB

challenges.title: IoT Telemetry and Anomaly Detection
challenges.scenario: A smart manufacturing company operates 50,000 sensors on factory floors worldwide. Each sensor reports temperature, pressure, and vibration readings every five seconds. The system must detect anomalies within 10 seconds of ingestion and immediately alert the maintenance team. Raw sensor data must be retained for 90 days to satisfy compliance requirements.

hints.text: At 50,000 sensors reporting every five seconds, you are handling 10,000 messages per second. Think about what is different about device connectivity compared to API calls — devices use lightweight protocols like MQTT, not HTTP. What AWS service is designed specifically for this?

AWS IoT Core acts as the managed MQTT broker for device connectivity and supports rule-based routing of ingested messages to other services. Think about how to separate the real-time anomaly detection path from the compliance retention path so neither interferes with the other.

IoT Core ingests device data over MQTT. IoT Rules route messages to Kinesis Data Streams. Lambda reads the stream for anomaly detection and publishes alerts to SNS. A parallel Kinesis Data Firehose path delivers raw data to S3 for 90-day retention with a lifecycle policy.

answer.summary: Devices connect to AWS IoT Core over MQTT. IoT Rules fan out each message to two paths: Kinesis Data Streams for real-time anomaly detection and Kinesis Data Firehose for durable retention in S3. A Lambda function consuming the Kinesis stream applies anomaly detection logic and publishes alerts to SNS, which notifies the maintenance team within 10 seconds.

optionalVariants.title: Amazon Timestream Instead of DynamoDB
optionalVariants.description: Replace DynamoDB with Amazon Timestream for native time-series queries and automatic hot-to-cold data tiering; reduces index complexity but adds a service to learn if your team is unfamiliar with time-series databases.

optionalVariants.title: SQS Instead of Kinesis
optionalVariants.description: Route IoT Core messages to SQS instead of Kinesis Data Streams for simpler queue-based processing; easier to operate but cannot replay messages after a failure and has a lower throughput ceiling.

tradeoffs: Kinesis Data Streams shard capacity must be planned for peak throughput — at 10,000 messages per second, shard count and record size determine the required capacity.
Lambda anomaly detection adds ~100ms per batch — ensure the Kinesis iterator age metric stays low to preserve the 10-second alert SLA.
IoT Core supports up to 100 messages per second per connection by default — use fleet indexing and batch operations for bulk device management.
answer.resilienceNotes: Kinesis retains records for 24 hours, allowing the anomaly detection Lambda to replay events after a failure. Firehose retries S3 delivery automatically on transient errors. IoT Core is multi-AZ and handles device reconnections transparently. S3 lifecycle policies enforce the 90-day retention window automatically.
answer.securityNotes: Each device authenticates to IoT Core using a unique X.509 certificate. IoT policies restrict each device to publishing only on its own topic prefix. KMS encrypts data at rest in Kinesis, S3, and any downstream stores. VPC endpoints prevent Lambda from accessing KMS and S3 over the public internet.
answer.whyItFits: High Volume: IoT Core and Kinesis Data Streams are designed for millions of concurrent device connections and thousands of messages per second without capacity pre-provisioning. Near Real-Time: the Kinesis → Lambda path delivers anomaly alerts within 5–10 seconds of sensor data being ingested.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    Sensors([50K Sensors])-->|MQTT|IoT[IoT Core]
    IoT-->|Rules|KDS[Kinesis Streams]
    KDS-->Lambda[Lambda]
    Lambda-->SNS[SNS Alert]
    KDS-->FH[Firehose]
    FH-->S3[(S3 90-day)]

challenges.title: Internal API Platform
challenges.scenario: A financial services company wants to expose a set of internal microservices as a unified API for their development teams. The API must enforce JWT-based authentication, apply per-consumer rate limiting, and provide auto-generated documentation. Response times must stay under 50ms at the 95th percentile. The operations team wants to avoid managing API infrastructure.

hints.text: Think about what AWS provides as a fully managed API platform that handles routing, authentication, and rate limiting without standing up any servers. What reduces the need for a custom authentication service in front of your microservices?

API Gateway has a built-in concept of usage plans and API keys for per-consumer rate limiting. For JWT validation, think about which authorizer type avoids a Lambda cold start on every request and caches the result of token validation.

Use API Gateway (HTTP API for lowest latency) with a Cognito JWT authorizer for authentication. Lambda proxy integrations back each microservice endpoint. Usage plans enforce per-consumer rate limits. API Gateway automatically generates an OpenAPI spec exportable for documentation.

answer.summary: API Gateway (HTTP API) acts as the unified front door. A Cognito JWT authorizer validates tokens at the edge without invoking a Lambda function, keeping authentication overhead minimal. Lambda proxy integrations route requests to each internal microservice. Usage plans with API keys enforce per-consumer rate limiting, and the built-in OpenAPI export provides auto-generated documentation.

optionalVariants.title: REST API Gateway for Richer Features
optionalVariants.description: Use REST API Gateway instead of HTTP API to get built-in request validation, response transformation, and per-method caching; adds 10–15 ms of latency and costs roughly 3× more per million requests.

optionalVariants.title: AWS AppSync for GraphQL Teams
optionalVariants.description: Replace API Gateway with AppSync if development teams prefer a GraphQL schema and want real-time subscriptions; adds complexity for teams unfamiliar with GraphQL resolvers and schema design.

tradeoffs: HTTP API is faster and cheaper than REST API but lacks some features — use REST API if you need request/response transformations or AWS service integrations.
Cognito JWT authorizer caches validation results by token; cache TTL must balance security (short TTL) against latency (longer TTL avoids repeated Cognito calls).
Lambda cold starts can push p99 latency above 50ms — use provisioned concurrency on latency-sensitive functions or consider direct VPC integrations for services that can accept HTTP directly.
answer.resilienceNotes: API Gateway is multi-AZ and automatically scales with no configuration. Lambda scales concurrently per request. CloudWatch alarms on 5XX error rate and integration latency provide early warning of downstream microservice degradation. WAF rules mitigate common API attack patterns including SQL injection and request flooding.
answer.securityNotes: Cognito User Pools issue and validate short-lived JWTs, eliminating the need to manage credentials for API consumers. AWS WAF sits in front of API Gateway to block malicious traffic before it reaches Lambda. Resource policies on the API restrict invocation to known internal IP ranges. CloudWatch Logs capture all API access for audit purposes.
answer.whyItFits: Low Latency: HTTP API Gateway has lower overhead than REST API; Cognito JWT validation happens at the edge before Lambda is invoked. Operational Simplicity: API Gateway, Cognito, Lambda, and CloudWatch are all fully managed — zero servers, zero patching, and zero custom authorisation code.
answer.diagram:
graph LR
    Dev([Developer])-->WAF[WAF]
    WAF-->APIGW[API Gateway]
    APIGW-->Auth[Cognito]
    APIGW-->L1[Lambda A]
    APIGW-->L2[Lambda B]
    APIGW-->L3[Lambda C]
    L1-->CW[CloudWatch]

challenges.title: Serverless Mobile Backend
challenges.scenario: A startup is building a mobile app for restaurant reviews. The backend must serve a REST API for user authentication, restaurant listings, and review submissions. Traffic is unpredictable — from 10 requests per minute during off-peak to 2,000 requests per second after a TV feature. The two-person team cannot manage any servers or containers.

hints.text: Think about what lets you run backend code without managing any servers or capacity planning. How do you handle authentication for mobile users without building your own sign-up and login system?

AWS has a managed user directory service that handles sign-up, sign-in, and JWT issuance for mobile apps out of the box. Pair it with a managed API gateway service to create HTTP endpoints that scale automatically.

Use Cognito User Pools for authentication, API Gateway (HTTP API) for routing, Lambda functions for business logic, and DynamoDB for data storage. CloudFront in front of API Gateway improves global latency and caching.

answer.summary: Cognito handles user sign-up, login, and JWT issuance. API Gateway HTTP API routes authenticated requests to Lambda functions implementing the REST endpoints. DynamoDB stores restaurants and reviews with single-digit millisecond reads. CloudFront sits in front for global edge caching of read-heavy listing endpoints.

optionalVariants.title: AppSync GraphQL API
optionalVariants.description: Replace API Gateway + Lambda with AWS AppSync for a GraphQL API with built-in real-time subscriptions; adds complexity for teams unfamiliar with GraphQL but reduces the number of Lambda functions needed.

optionalVariants.title: Aurora Serverless for Relational Data
optionalVariants.description: Use Aurora Serverless v2 instead of DynamoDB if the data model requires complex joins and relational queries; adds cost during idle periods but simplifies reporting queries.

tradeoffs: DynamoDB requires upfront data modelling with access patterns in mind — retrofitting a new query pattern later may require a secondary index or table redesign.
Lambda cold starts can add 200–500ms on the first request after idle — use provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive endpoints if needed.
CloudFront caching requires careful cache-key design to avoid serving stale review data while still caching restaurant listings effectively.
answer.resilienceNotes: All services are multi-AZ by default. DynamoDB provides continuous backups with point-in-time recovery. Lambda automatically retries on transient failures. CloudFront's edge locations provide natural redundancy against regional issues for cached content.
answer.securityNotes: Cognito issues short-lived JWTs validated at the API Gateway layer before any Lambda code executes. DynamoDB encryption at rest is enabled by default. Lambda functions use least-privilege IAM roles. API Gateway resource policies can restrict access to known mobile app user agents during development.
answer.whyItFits: Operational Simplicity: Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, and DynamoDB are all fully managed — zero servers to provision, patch, or scale. Cost Sensitive: every component is pay-per-use with generous free tiers — the system costs pennies during low traffic and scales automatically during viral spikes.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    App([Mobile App])-->CF[CloudFront]
    CF-->APIGW[API Gateway]
    APIGW-->Auth[Cognito]
    APIGW-->L1[Lambda: Listings]
    APIGW-->L2[Lambda: Reviews]
    APIGW-->L3[Lambda: Users]
    L1-->DDB[(DynamoDB)]
    L2-->DDB
    L3-->DDB

challenges.title: Retail Data Lake Analytics
challenges.scenario: A national retail chain wants to consolidate data from 500 stores — point-of-sale transactions, inventory counts, and customer loyalty events — into a central analytics platform. Business analysts need to run SQL queries across the full dataset and build dashboards without waiting for the data engineering team. The dataset grows by 50 GB daily and must be queryable within one hour of arrival.

hints.text: Think about where you would land and store large volumes of data cheaply so that it survives indefinitely and can be queried without loading it into a traditional database. What is the most cost-effective durable storage on AWS?
AWS has a managed ETL service that can crawl raw data, infer schemas, and register them in a central catalogue — making your S3 data queryable by standard SQL tools without a database cluster.

Store raw data in S3. Use Glue crawlers and ETL jobs to clean, partition, and register data in the Glue Data Catalog. Athena runs serverless SQL queries directly on S3. QuickSight connects to Athena for dashboard visualisation. Lake Formation governs fine-grained access control.

answer.summary: Raw store data lands in S3 via Kinesis Firehose (streaming) or S3 batch uploads (daily). Glue ETL jobs clean, partition by date and store, and write Parquet files back to S3. The Glue Data Catalog registers schemas so Athena can run SQL queries directly against S3. QuickSight connects to Athena for interactive dashboards accessible to business analysts. Lake Formation provides column-level access control.

optionalVariants.title: Redshift Serverless for Complex Analytics
optionalVariants.description: Add Redshift Serverless for sub-second queries on frequently accessed data; higher cost than Athena but dramatically faster for complex joins across large tables.

optionalVariants.title: Apache Spark on EMR Serverless
optionalVariants.description: Run Spark jobs on EMR Serverless instead of Glue ETL for teams with existing PySpark expertise; more flexible but requires more configuration and monitoring.

tradeoffs: Athena charges per TB scanned — partitioning by date and store and using columnar formats like Parquet is essential to control costs as the dataset grows.
Glue ETL job startup time is 1–2 minutes per run — batch multiple files into fewer larger jobs rather than triggering per-file.
QuickSight SPICE in-memory cache accelerates dashboards but requires periodic refresh scheduling to keep data current.
answer.resilienceNotes: S3 provides eleven nines of durability. Glue jobs can be configured with automatic retries on failure. Athena queries are stateless and can be retried immediately. QuickSight SPICE datasets are replicated across multiple AZs.
answer.securityNotes: Lake Formation enforces column-level and row-level access controls so analysts see only data they are authorised to access. S3 bucket policies enforce encryption at rest with KMS. Glue jobs run in a private VPC with no internet access. Athena workgroup settings enforce query result encryption and scan limits.
answer.whyItFits: High Volume: S3 scales infinitely for storage; Glue and Athena process terabytes without provisioning clusters. Cost Sensitive: S3 storage is pennies per GB; Athena charges only per query; Glue charges per DPU-minute — there are no idle clusters running overnight.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    Stores([500 Stores])-->FH[Kinesis Firehose]
    Stores-->Batch[S3 Batch Upload]
    FH-->S3[(S3 Data Lake)]
    Batch-->S3
    S3-->Glue[Glue ETL]
    Glue-->S3
    S3-->Cat[Glue Catalog]
    Cat-->Athena[Athena]
    Athena-->QS[QuickSight]
    S3-->LF[Lake Formation]

challenges.title: Real-Time Collaborative Workspace
challenges.scenario: A productivity startup is building a collaborative document editor where up to 50 users can edit the same document simultaneously. Every keystroke must be visible to all participants within 200 milliseconds. The system must handle 10,000 concurrent editing sessions at peak. The team wants minimal infrastructure to manage.

hints.text: Traditional request-response APIs poll for changes and introduce latency. What communication protocol lets the server push updates to all connected clients instantly without polling?
API Gateway supports WebSocket APIs that maintain persistent connections to clients. Think about how you would track which connections belong to the same document and broadcast changes efficiently.

Use API Gateway WebSocket API for persistent connections. Lambda handles connect, disconnect, and message events. DynamoDB stores connection mappings and document state. ElastiCache (Redis) provides sub-millisecond pub/sub for broadcasting edits to all participants in a document room.

answer.summary: Clients connect via API Gateway WebSocket API, which maintains persistent bidirectional connections. Lambda functions handle connection lifecycle and message routing. DynamoDB stores the connection-to-document mapping and persists document state. ElastiCache (Redis) provides sub-millisecond pub/sub to fan out edits to all participants in the same document room, keeping broadcast latency under 200ms.

optionalVariants.title: AWS AppSync Subscriptions
optionalVariants.description: Use AppSync with real-time subscriptions instead of API Gateway WebSocket for simpler client-side integration; adds a GraphQL dependency but eliminates manual connection management code.

optionalVariants.title: IoT Core MQTT for Fan-Out
optionalVariants.description: Use IoT Core as the pub/sub layer instead of ElastiCache — each document becomes an MQTT topic; eliminates cache infrastructure but adds IoT Core message costs and a 128KB payload limit.

tradeoffs: API Gateway WebSocket has a 29-second idle timeout — implement heartbeat pings to keep connections alive during idle editing periods.
ElastiCache requires a VPC and adds a fixed hourly cost — size the cluster for peak concurrent sessions, not average, to avoid latency spikes.
DynamoDB write throughput for document state must handle 50 users × keystroke rate — use batch writes and client-side buffering to reduce write volume.
answer.resilienceNotes: API Gateway WebSocket connections automatically reconnect on transient failures. DynamoDB provides point-in-time recovery for document state. ElastiCache Multi-AZ provides automatic failover. Lambda retries on transient errors with configurable backoff.
answer.securityNotes: API Gateway authorizers validate JWT tokens on the WebSocket $connect route before establishing the connection. DynamoDB encryption at rest protects document content. ElastiCache in-transit encryption secures internal pub/sub traffic. Lambda functions use least-privilege IAM roles scoped to their specific DynamoDB tables and ElastiCache clusters.
answer.whyItFits: Low Latency: ElastiCache Redis pub/sub delivers under 1ms fan-out; API Gateway WebSocket eliminates HTTP polling overhead. Near Real-Time: the push-based architecture ensures edits are visible to all participants within 200ms of being made.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    U1([User])-->|WebSocket|APIGW[API Gateway WebSocket]
    U2([User])-->|WebSocket|APIGW
    APIGW-->LConn[Lambda: Connect]
    APIGW-->LMsg[Lambda: Message]
    APIGW-->LDisc[Lambda: Disconnect]
    LConn-->DDB[(DynamoDB)]
    LMsg-->DDB
    LMsg-->Cache[ElastiCache Redis]
    LMsg-->|Broadcast|APIGW

challenges.title: Multi-Region Disaster Recovery
challenges.scenario: A B2B SaaS company provides a contract management platform used by enterprise clients during business hours worldwide. Their SLA guarantees 99.99% availability and a recovery time objective (RTO) of under 15 minutes. The platform currently runs in a single AWS region. Management has approved a budget-conscious approach that balances resilience against cost — they do not want to pay for full active-active capacity in a second region at all times.

hints.text: Think about the spectrum of DR strategies — backup & restore, pilot light, warm standby, and active-active. Which strategy meets a 15-minute RTO without paying for full capacity in two regions?

A warm standby strategy runs a scaled-down copy of the production stack in a second region. AWS has a managed database feature that replicates data continuously across regions with sub-second lag — what is it called?

Use Aurora Global Database for cross-region replication with <1 second lag. ECS Fargate in the DR region runs a minimal task count (warm standby). Route 53 health checks detect primary region failure and fail over DNS. S3 Cross-Region Replication keeps static assets in sync. On failover, auto-scaling policies in the DR region scale up to full capacity.

answer.summary: The primary region runs the full production stack on ECS Fargate behind an ALB, with Aurora as the database and S3 for static assets. A warm standby in the DR region runs a minimal ECS task count with Aurora Global Database providing continuous replication (<1 second lag). Route 53 health checks monitor the primary and automatically fail over DNS within 60 seconds. On failover, ECS auto-scaling in the DR region scales to production capacity within minutes, meeting the 15-minute RTO.

optionalVariants.title: Pilot Light Instead of Warm Standby
optionalVariants.description: Keep only the database replica running in the DR region and launch compute on demand during failover; reduces ongoing DR cost by 60% but increases RTO to 20–30 minutes for compute provisioning.

optionalVariants.title: Active-Active with DynamoDB Global Tables
optionalVariants.description: Replace Aurora with DynamoDB Global Tables for active-active multi-region writes; eliminates the promote-replica step during failover but requires redesigning the data model for eventual consistency.

tradeoffs: Aurora Global Database requires promoting the DR replica to a standalone writer during failover — this takes 1–2 minutes and is the single largest contributor to RTO.
Running a minimal warm standby in the DR region costs roughly 20% of primary region spend — pilot light is cheaper but risks exceeding the 15-minute RTO.
Route 53 DNS TTL must be set low (60 seconds) to minimise failover detection time, which slightly increases DNS query costs.
S3 Cross-Region Replication is asynchronous — files uploaded in the seconds before a failure may not yet be in the DR region.
answer.resilienceNotes: Route 53 health checks run every 10 seconds from multiple locations. Aurora Global Database replication lag is typically under 1 second. ECS auto-scaling reacts to the increased load within 2–3 minutes. CloudFormation stack sets ensure both regions have identical infrastructure definitions. S3 CRR provides eventual consistency with typical lag under 15 minutes for new objects.
answer.securityNotes: Aurora encryption at rest with KMS is replicated to the DR region using a separate region-specific KMS key. IAM roles and policies are deployed identically to both regions via CloudFormation StackSets. VPC configurations in both regions use identical CIDR ranges connected via Transit Gateway for secure cross-region communication during failover testing. Secrets Manager replicates database credentials to the DR region.
answer.whyItFits: Multi-Region Resilience: warm standby with Aurora Global Database and Route 53 failover delivers an RTO under 15 minutes with automated detection and DNS switchover. Cost Sensitive: running minimal compute in the DR region costs a fraction of active-active while still meeting the availability SLA.
answer.diagram:
graph TD
    Users([Users])-->R53[Route 53 Failover]
    R53-->ALB1[ALB Primary]
    R53-->ALB2[ALB DR]
    ALB1-->ECS1[ECS Fargate]
    ALB2-->ECS2[ECS Fargate Minimal]
    ECS1-->Aurora1[(Aurora Primary)]
    ECS2-->Aurora2[(Aurora Replica)]
    Aurora1-->|Global DB|Aurora2
    ECS1-->S3P[(S3)]
    S3P-->|CRR|S3D[(S3 Replica)]
